title: 5. Multi-threading — when parallel helps (and when it hurts)
description: I/O fan-out with a bound; keep ranking serial; admit only N diagnoses at once.
story:
Threads are workers. If four workers each wait on a slow connector, total time is about the slowest wait — not four times one wait. That is I/O parallelism.

If four workers all thrash the same CPU-heavy ranking with shared mutable state, you get bugs and little speedup (Python GIL + race conditions).

Rules for this platform:
• EXTRACT connectors: parallel OK (bound the pool, e.g. max_workers=4).
• TRANSFORM / Bayes ranking: serial, deterministic.
• ADMISSION: only N diagnoses in flight (semaphore / Redis counter) so Neo4j is not crushed.
one_liner: Parallel I/O with a bound; serial transform; limit in-flight diagnoses.
why_it_matters: Connector fan-out is mostly waiting on network.
Unbounded threads open too many DB sessions under load.
say_aloud: I parallelize independent I/O, not the core ranking math.
Admission control caps concurrent expensive diagnose calls.
Shared mutable state across threads needs locks or immutability.

cheat_sheet.meaning: Heavy compute — threads help less in CPython
CPython lock — pure-Python CPU parallelism limited

beats.title: Parallel extract with a bound
beats.narrative: Four connectors fetch at once; results collected; then serial build.
beats.code:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

def fetch_all(fetch_fns: list, max_workers: int = 4) -> list:
    # Bound the pool — protects sockets and the source systems
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as pool:
        return list(pool.map(lambda fn: fn(), fetch_fns))

records = fetch_all([fetch_pim, fetch_fsm, fetch_claims, fetch_crm])
catalog = build_abox_serial(records)   # serial on purpose
beats.say_after: map runs functions concurrently; build stays single-threaded.

annotations.note: Context manager shuts workers down cleanly.
Deterministic merge after all I/O returns.

line_quiz.why: Unbounded pools exhaust FDs and backends.

fill_blanks.template:
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers={{n}}) as pool:
    return list(pool.{{m}}(lambda fn: fn(), fetch_fns))

pro_tips: GOTCHA: CPython's GIL means threads help I/O-bound waits, NOT CPU-bound work — use processes for CPU parallelism (Python docs).
TRICK: parallel EXTRACT (I/O) is safe; parallel TRANSFORM often isn't — shared mutable state races.
TRICK: prefer concurrent.futures ThreadPoolExecutor.map over manual threads; always cap max_workers.

beats.title: Admission: only N diagnoses at once
beats.say_after: Always release in finally so a crash does not leak a slot.

line_quiz.answer: Release slot even if fn raises
choices: Release slot even if fn raises
line_quiz.why: Otherwise in_flight leaks and system stays 'full'.

concepts.say_aloud: I/O fan-out with a worker ceiling.

concepts.definition: Build ABox and rank deterministically in one flow.
concepts.say_aloud: No race conditions in confidence merges.

common_mistakes: Spawning a thread per HTTP request without a pool.
Sharing a non-thread-safe dict across workers without locks.
final_boss: Explain GIL in one honest sentence.
